name: Maldives
excerpt: A sultanate since the 12th century, the Maldives became a British protectorate in 1887 and a republic in 1968, three years after independence.

quick_facts.languages: Dhivehi (official, closely related to Sinhala, script derived from Arabic), English (spoken by most government officials)
quick_facts.ethnic_groups: homogeneous mixture of Sinhalese, Dravidian, Arab, Australasian, and African resulting from historical changes in regional hegemony over marine trade routes
quick_facts.climate: tropical; hot, humid; dry, northeast monsoon (November to March); rainy, southwest monsoon (June to August)
quick_facts.terrain: flat coral atolls, with white sandy beaches; sits atop the submarine volcanic Chagos-Laccadive Ridge
quick_facts.major_exports: fish, aircraft, refined petroleum, scrap iron, natural gas (2023)

summaries.geography_summary: smallest Asian country; archipelago of 1,190 coral islands grouped into 26 atolls (200 inhabited islands, plus 80 islands with tourist resorts); strategic location along major sea lanes in Indian Ocean
summaries.history_summary: A sultanate since the 12th century, the Maldives became a British protectorate in 1887 and a republic in 1968, three years after independence. President Maumoon Abdul GAYOOM dominated Maldives' political scene for 30 years, elected to six successive terms by single-party referendums. After political demonstrations in the capital Male in 2003, GAYOOM and his government pledged to embark upon a process of liberalization and democratic reforms, including a more representative political system and expanded political freedoms. Political parties were legalized in 2005. In 2008, a constituent assembly -- termed the "Special Majlis" -- finalized a new constitution ratified by GAYOOM. The first-ever presidential elections under a multi-candidate, multi-party system were held later that year. GAYOOM was defeated in a runoff by Mohamed NASHEED, a political activist whom the regime had jailed several years earlier. In 2012, after several weeks of street protests in response to a top judge's arrest, NASHEED resigned the presidency and handed over power to Vice President Mohammed WAHEED Hassan Maniku. A government-appointed Commission of National Inquiry concluded that there was no evidence of a coup, but NASHEED contended that police and military personnel forced him to resign. NASHEED, WAHEED, and Abdulla YAMEEN Abdul Gayoom ran in the 2013 elections with YAMEEN ultimately winning the presidency after three rounds of voting. In 2018, YAMEEN lost his reelection bid to parliamentarian Ibrahim Mohamed SOLIH. YAMEEN was arrested and jailed in 2022 on corruption charges. Maldives' fourth democratic election was held in September 2023. The winner, Male City Mayor Dr. Mohamed MUIZZU, campaigned on a platform of Maldivian sovereignty, vowing to remove Indian military personnel from the country. MUIZZU represents a joint Progressive Pary of Maldives and People's National Congress (PPM/PNC) coalition.
summaries.government_summary: presidential republic. Independence: 26 July 1965 (from the UK)
summaries.economy_summary: upper middle-income Indian Ocean island economy; major tourism, fishing, and shipping industries; high public debt; systemic corruption; crippled by COVID-19; ongoing deflation; poverty has tripled since pandemic began

suggested_reading: CIA World Factbook data (archived via factbook.json) | https://github.com/factbook/factbook.json

prayer_and_mission.prayer_intro: The Maldives is officially and exclusively Sunni Muslim, with no legal provision for any other religion and citizenship itself tied to Islamic identity. Pray for gospel access in one of the most religiously restricted nations in the world.
prayer_points: Pray for any Maldivians who have come to faith in Christ, who must worship in complete secrecy, to be protected.
Pray for open doors for the gospel in a nation where conversion is effectively illegal.
Pray for gospel witness among the large foreign tourism workforce, who have freedoms Maldivian citizens do not.
prayer_and_mission.mission_emphasis: The Maldives' constitutional requirement that citizens be Muslim makes it one of the most closed nations in the world to the gospel. Ongoing needs include discreet prayer for any hidden believers and continued intercession for greater openness.
